summary: DeVault recounts debugging a segfault in scdoc caused by passing a UCS-32 codepoint to glibc's isalnum function. He traces the crash through glibc's labyrinthine macro-heavy implementation, which uses lookup tables, thread-local storage, locale support, and pointer dereferences — any of which can segfault on out-of-range input. He contrasts this with musl libc's simple, three-function implementation that can never segfault regardless of input. While acknowledging his code violated the C spec, he argues that a character classification function should never crash a program.
key_points: glibc's isalnum implementation involves five macros, endianness checks, a lookup table, thread-local storage, and two pointer dereferences — making it possible to segfault on unexpected input
musl libc's isalnum is a simple arithmetic comparison that works safely for any input value
The C spec says isalnum input must be representable as unsigned char or EOF, but DeVault argues that violating this precondition should not cause a crash
The debugging journey through glibc's ctype.h header illustrates the accumulated complexity of GNU's coding style
money_quotes: As expected, for any value of c, isalnum will never segfault. Because why the fuck would isalnum segfault?
When opening this header, you're greeted with the typical GNU bullshit, but let's trudge through and grep for isalnum.
Maybe because the spec says you can does not mean you should.

Maybe, just maybe, the behavior of this function should not depend on five macros, whether or not you're using a C++ compiler, the endianness of your machine, a look-up table, thread-local storage, and two pointer dereferences.
That is just so, super cool of you, glibc. I just love dealing with locales.
Oh, for fuck's sake. Whatever, let's move on and just assume this is a magic number.
key_insight: Simple, obvious implementations that can never crash are superior to spec-lawyering optimizations that turn trivial character checks into segfault landmines.
